FAQ: My laptop won't turn on — what should I try before contacting support?

Before contacting Omnia Retail support, please try the following steps in order. Most 'laptop won't turn on' issues can be resolved without returning the device.

STEP 1: Check the basics
- Ensure the charger is properly connected at both the laptop end and the wall socket
- Try a different wall socket — the socket itself may be faulty
- If using a USB-C charger or dock, ensure it is rated for your laptop's wattage (check your laptop's specifications — many require 65W or 90W minimum to charge and run simultaneously)
- Look for any indicator LEDs on the laptop — a charging LED, power LED, or battery LED can tell you whether the laptop is receiving power

STEP 2: Perform a hard reset
This clears any residual electrical charge that can prevent startup. Method varies by laptop:
- Most Windows laptops: hold the power button for 15–20 seconds, release, then press normally to power on
- MacBooks: hold the power button for 10 seconds. On M-series Macs: press and hold power button until the startup options screen appears, or for 10 seconds to force shutdown if stuck
- If your laptop has a removable battery (older models): remove battery, hold power button for 10 seconds, reinsert battery, attempt power on

STEP 3: Check for display issues (laptop may be on but screen blank)
- Look very closely at the screen — do you see a very faint image? If so, the backlight may have failed (the laptop is on but the screen backlight is not)
- Connect to an external monitor using HDMI or USB-C DisplayPort. If the external monitor shows the desktop, the laptop's internal display has a fault — contact support
- Press the brightness keys (Fn + brightness up) to rule out the screen simply being set to minimum brightness

STEP 4: Boot from charging only
- Allow the laptop to charge for 30 minutes before attempting to power on — if the battery was deeply discharged below a safe threshold, it needs minimum charge before it can power the startup circuit

STEP 5: Listen and observe
- On startup attempt: do you hear fan spin, keyboard backlights flash, or any beep codes? Note these for the support agent — beep codes often indicate specific hardware faults

If none of these steps resolve the issue, please contact Omnia Retail support at support@omniaretail.ie or 01-XXX-XXXX with your order number and a description of what you observed in each step above. This information helps us resolve your issue faster.

FAQ: What warranty coverage do I get when I buy from Omnia Retail?

All products sold by Omnia Retail come with two distinct layers of warranty protection:

1. STATUTORY RIGHTS (Irish and EU Law)
Under the Consumer Rights Act 2022 (Ireland), which implements the EU Sale of Goods Directive (2019/771/EU), you have a statutory 2-year right to remedies for any product that is not in conformity with the contract at the time of delivery. This means:
- In the first 12 months: the defect is presumed to have existed at the time of sale. You do not need to prove the product was faulty — Omnia Retail must prove it was not, or offer a remedy.
- In months 13–24: you may need to demonstrate that a defect is due to a manufacturing fault rather than normal wear and misuse, but your rights remain in place.

Remedies available under statutory rights: repair, replacement, or (where repair/replacement is not possible or takes too long) a price reduction or full refund. Omnia Retail will always first attempt repair or replacement; refund is a last resort under the legislation.

These statutory rights are in addition to any manufacturer warranty and cannot be contracted away.

2. MANUFACTURER WARRANTY
Most products also come with a separate manufacturer warranty, typically 1–3 years depending on the brand and product. Manufacturer warranties may offer advantages over statutory rights, such as: faster direct-to-manufacturer repair, advance replacement programmes (new unit dispatched before the faulty unit is returned), and international coverage. Manufacturer warranty terms vary — key details for common brands:

- Dell: 1-year basic onsite / ProSupport varies by product
- Lenovo: 1-year depot or onsite depending on product line
- HP: 1-year with optional Care Pack extensions
- Samsung (monitors/storage): 3-year manufacturer warranty on most products
- Apple: 1-year hardware warranty + 90 days telephone support (AppleCare+ available)
- LG (monitors): 3-year on most display products

3. WHAT IS NOT COVERED
Neither statutory nor manufacturer warranties cover: accidental damage (drops, liquid spills, screen cracks); damage caused by misuse, modification, or repair by non-authorised service centres; normal wear and tear (battery capacity degradation over time is normal and not a warranty fault unless it falls below manufacturer thresholds, typically 80% at 1 year).

4. HOW TO MAKE A WARRANTY CLAIM
Contact Omnia Retail support first — we will advise whether to process through us or directly with the manufacturer, depending on which route is faster and more beneficial for you. Have ready: your order number or proof of purchase, product serial number (usually on a sticker on the base of the device), and a clear description of the fault.

Omnia Retail will never instruct you to contact the manufacturer directly in a way that undermines your statutory rights. Your rights are with us as the retailer, and we will always support you in exercising them.

FAQ: What is the difference between Intel Core i5, i7, and i9 processors?

Intel's Core processor numbering (i3, i5, i7, i9) indicates relative performance tier within a generation. Here's a practical guide for choosing the right processor for your needs:

Core i3 — Entry level. Suitable for: basic web browsing, email, word processing, streaming media. Not suitable for: video editing, gaming, running multiple demanding applications. Typically found in budget laptops under €600.

Core i5 — Mid-range, the most popular tier. Suitable for: business productivity (Microsoft 365, video conferencing, web research), light photo editing, casual gaming, students across most disciplines. Excellent value for most users. The i5 in the current 12th and 13th generation (e.g. i5-1235U, i5-1345U) is significantly more powerful than i5s from 4–5 years ago — don't dismiss it.

Core i7 — Upper-mid range. Suitable for: video editing (1080p/4K), software development, architecture and CAD, gaming laptops, heavy multitasking, data science. The i7 will handle demanding applications comfortably and future-proof your laptop for longer.

Core i9 — Enthusiast/professional tier. Suitable for: professional video production, 3D rendering, machine learning workloads, high-end gaming. Typically found in high-performance laptops (ROG, Spectre, MacBook Pro equivalent territory). Generates significantly more heat than i5/i7 — expect louder fans in sustained workloads.

Important: The suffix letter matters.
- 'U' suffix (e.g. i7-1355U): Ultra-low power, optimised for battery life. Used in thin ultrabooks. Good performance, excellent efficiency.
- 'P' suffix (e.g. i7-1360P): Performance-optimised for thin-and-light. Balanced between U and H.
- 'H' suffix (e.g. i7-13700H): High performance, typically in thicker laptops. Maximum performance for creative and gaming workloads. Higher power draw, shorter battery life.
- 'HX' suffix: Desktop-grade performance in a laptop. Found in workstation-class machines.

Also Important — Generation numbers: A 12th or 13th generation i5 will often outperform a 10th generation i7. Always check the generation (the first two digits after the dash: i7-13700H = 13th generation).

Apple Silicon alternative: If you're considering a Mac, Apple's M-series chips (M2, M3) are not directly comparable to Intel tiers but generally match or exceed i7/i9 performance in most tasks while using significantly less power.

FAQ: What monitor size and resolution should I choose?

Choosing monitor size and resolution is about matching pixel density, viewing distance, and use case. Here is a practical guide:

SIZE GUIDE
24-inch: Ideal for a standard desk where you sit 50–65cm from the screen. Compact, easy on neck with no need to pan. Great for office work, coding, general use.
27-inch: The most popular professional size. Comfortable at 60–80cm. Gives noticeably more workspace than 24-inch. The sweet spot for most home office and creative setups.
32-inch: Best for those who want an immersive experience or sit further from the screen (80–100cm). Excellent for video editing timelines, large spreadsheets. Can feel like too much at close range.
34-inch Ultrawide (21:9): Replaces a dual-monitor setup for many users. Excellent for multitasking — two windows side-by-side with no bezel gap. Not all applications fully support ultrawide ratios.

RESOLUTION GUIDE
1080p (Full HD, 1920×1080): Appropriate for 24-inch screens. Looks fine at typical viewing distances. Appears soft on 27-inch+ at close range — text won't be as crisp.
1440p (QHD/2K, 2560×1440): The recommended resolution for 27-inch monitors. Sharp, comfortable pixel density, less GPU demand than 4K. Excellent for both work and gaming.
4K (UHD, 3840×2160): Essential if you work with 4K video, high-resolution photo editing, or simply want the sharpest possible text. Perfect for 27-inch professional use. Requires scaling (Windows: 150%, macOS: Retina default) for comfortable text size.
4K on 32-inch: Excellent for video editors and photographers. The extra space is useful for timeline work.

COLOUR PANEL TECHNOLOGY
IPS: Best colour accuracy, wide viewing angles. Recommended for design work, photo/video editing, and anyone viewing from different angles.
VA: Higher contrast ratio, good for dark rooms and multimedia. Slight colour shift at extreme angles.
OLED: Best-in-class contrast and colour vibrancy. Premium price. Excellent for creative professionals; burn-in risk with static UI elements (managed by screen savers and pixel refresh features on modern panels).

Omnia Retail Recommendation: For most home office users — 27-inch 1440p IPS. For photographers and video editors — 27-inch 4K IPS or OLED. For gaming alongside work — 27-inch 1440p with 144Hz+ refresh rate.

FAQ: What is the difference between HDD, SATA SSD, and NVMe SSD storage?

Storage technology has evolved significantly over the past decade. Here's what you need to know to choose the right storage for your needs:

HDD (Hard Disk Drive)
How it works: Spinning magnetic platters with a read/write head — mechanical storage.
Speed: ~100–200 MB/s sequential read/write.
Best for: Large-capacity bulk storage at low cost — external backup drives, NAS systems, archival storage. Not recommended as a primary drive in a new laptop or desktop in 2024.
Cost per GB: Cheapest — typically €0.02–0.04/GB.
Durability: Vulnerable to shocks and drops (moving parts). Not suitable for portable use without protective cases.

SATA SSD (Solid State Drive, SATA interface)
How it works: NAND flash memory chips, connected via the SATA III bus (same interface as older HDDs).
Speed: ~500–550 MB/s sequential read, ~450–520 MB/s sequential write. Approximately 3–5× faster than HDD.
Best for: Budget laptop upgrades (replacing an old HDD), desktop secondary drives, external portable drives. Still a significant improvement over HDD for general computing.
Cost per GB: ~€0.07–0.12/GB.
Durability: No moving parts — much more shock-resistant than HDD.

NVMe SSD (Non-Volatile Memory Express, PCIe interface)
How it works: Flash memory chips communicating directly via PCIe lanes — no SATA bottleneck.
Speed: Gen 3 NVMe: ~3,000–3,500 MB/s read / ~2,000–3,000 MB/s write. Gen 4 NVMe: ~5,000–7,000 MB/s read / ~4,000–6,000 MB/s write. Gen 5 NVMe: up to 12,000 MB/s (cutting edge as of 2024).
Best for: Primary drive in any modern laptop or desktop. Operating system, applications, and active project files benefit enormously from NVMe speeds. Video editors, developers, and power users should always use NVMe as their primary drive.
Cost per GB: ~€0.09–0.18/GB (premium over SATA SSD narrowing rapidly).
Form factors: M.2 2280 (most common laptop/desktop), M.2 2242 (smaller laptops), PCIe add-in card (desktops).

Practical Impact on Daily Use:
Boot times: HDD: 45–90 seconds. SATA SSD: 15–25 seconds. NVMe: 8–15 seconds.
App launch: NVMe feels instantaneous for most applications vs. noticeable wait on HDD.
Large file operations: NVMe can move a 10GB video in under 3 seconds; HDD takes over a minute.

Omnia Retail Recommendation: Choose NVMe SSD as your primary drive in any new purchase. Use HDD or SATA SSD for secondary/external backup storage where cost per GB matters more than speed.

FAQ: My monitor is displaying the wrong colours — how do I fix it?

Colour issues on a monitor can have several causes. Work through the following steps to diagnose and resolve:

STEP 1: Check colour temperature and preset mode
Most monitors ship in a mode unsuitable for accurate colour (e.g. 'Vivid', 'Game', or 'Cinema' mode which boost saturation and contrast artificially). Access your monitor's OSD (On-Screen Display) menu using the buttons on the monitor bezel. Look for: Picture Mode, Colour Temperature, or Preset Mode. For accurate colour: select 'sRGB' mode, or if not available, 'Standard'. Set Colour Temperature to 6500K. This alone resolves most colour complaint cases.

STEP 2: Check cable type
HDMI cables transmit colour in 'limited range' (16–235) by default on many systems, causing washed-out blacks and whites. DisplayPort transmits full range (0–255) by default. If using HDMI: in Windows, go to Display Settings > Advanced Display Settings > Display Adapter Properties > Colour Management, or in NVIDIA/AMD control panel set Output Dynamic Range to 'Full'. If using a Mac with HDMI: this is managed automatically in macOS Monterey and later.

STEP 3: Check your operating system colour profile
Windows: Go to Settings > System > Display > Advanced display settings > Colour profile. Ensure the correct ICC profile for your monitor is applied. Many monitors include a disc or downloadable ICC profile — check the manufacturer's support page.
macOS: Go to System Settings > Displays > Colour Profile. Try different profiles or click 'Calibrate' for a manual calibration walkthrough.

STEP 4: Check GPU colour output settings
NVIDIA users: Open NVIDIA Control Panel > Display > Adjust desktop colour settings. Ensure 'Use NVIDIA settings' is selected. Set Output Colour Format to RGB, Output Colour Depth to 8bpc or 10bpc, and Output Dynamic Range to Full.
AMD users: Open AMD Radeon Software > Display. Check Pixel Format is set to RGB 4:4:4 Pixel Format PC Standard (Full RGB).

STEP 5: Factory reset the monitor
If colour is still incorrect after the above steps, perform a factory reset via the OSD menu (usually under Setup or System options). This returns all monitor settings to default.

STEP 6: Hardware fault
If the display shows a consistent colour cast (e.g. everything appears pink, green, or yellow) that persists after reset and across multiple cables and inputs, this may indicate a hardware fault with the panel or internal display electronics. Contact Omnia Retail support with your order details and a photo of the display issue.

FAQ: What accessories do I need for a productive home office setup?

Building a comfortable, productive home office doesn't require spending a fortune. Here is a practical guide to the most impactful upgrades, in order of priority:

1. EXTERNAL MONITOR (Highest Impact)
Even if your laptop screen is excellent, an external monitor dramatically increases your usable workspace. A 27-inch 1440p IPS monitor (€250–400) gives you a second full screen for reference documents, email, or communication while your primary work stays on the laptop. If budget is tight, a 24-inch 1080p IPS (€120–180) is a significant improvement over no second screen. See our monitor size and resolution FAQ for detailed guidance.

2. EXTERNAL KEYBOARD AND MOUSE
Laptop keyboards are functional but not designed for 8-hour days. An external keyboard allows your laptop screen to be elevated (see below) while you type at a comfortable angle. Recommended for most users: a wireless keyboard and mouse combo (Logitech MK470 Slim, €69) for desk tidiness. For heavy typists: a mechanical keyboard (Keychron K3 or K8) dramatically reduces typing fatigue.

3. LAPTOP STAND OR MONITOR ARM
Laptop screens used flat on a desk force your neck down — this causes significant neck and shoulder strain over time. A laptop stand (Nexstand K2 at €35, or Rain Design mStand for MacBooks at €49) raises the screen to eye level. Pair with an external keyboard and mouse. This is one of the cheapest, highest-impact ergonomic investments available.

4. USB HUB OR DOCK
Modern laptops often have too few ports. A powered USB-C hub (€30–60) adds USB-A ports, HDMI, SD card reader, and sometimes Ethernet. For a more powerful solution: a Thunderbolt 4 dock (CalDigit TS4, €299) connects everything — dual monitors, USB devices, Ethernet, audio — through one cable to your laptop.

5. WEBCAM
If you use video calls regularly, a dedicated external webcam (Logitech C920 at €79, or Logitech Brio 4K at €149) is a substantial upgrade over most laptop webcams, which are 720p in most non-premium models.

6. HEADSET OR SPEAKERS
For calls: a wireless headset (Jabra Evolve2 55, €289; or Poly Voyager Focus 2, €199) with active noise cancellation keeps your calls private in shared spaces and significantly improves call audio quality. Budget option: any USB headset with a microphone (~€25–50).

7. LIGHTING
A ring light or desk lamp with consistent, non-flickering LED illumination improves your on-camera appearance dramatically and reduces eye strain when working in darker environments.

Omnia Retail Tip: If you're starting from nothing, prioritise in this order: monitor → stand + keyboard/mouse → dock/hub → webcam. You'll notice diminishing returns after the first three items.

FAQ: My laptop is running slowly — what can I do to speed it up?

A slow laptop is one of the most common support questions we receive. Before assuming you need a new machine, try the following steps — many users find a significant improvement without spending anything.

STEP 1: Check what is using your resources
Windows: Press Ctrl+Shift+Esc to open Task Manager. Click 'More details', then sort by CPU and Memory columns to see what's consuming resources. A process at 80–100% CPU consistently indicates either a runaway process or a system under genuine heavy load.
macOS: Open Activity Monitor (Applications > Utilities > Activity Monitor). Check CPU and Memory tabs.

STEP 2: Manage startup programmes
Many applications add themselves to startup without asking. These slow your boot time and run in the background consuming RAM.
Windows 11: Settings > Apps > Startup. Disable anything non-essential.
macOS: System Settings > General > Login Items. Remove unused apps.

STEP 3: Check available storage
Systems with less than 10–15% free storage often slow down significantly (Windows needs space for virtual memory paging; both Windows and macOS write temporary files constantly).
Windows: Settings > System > Storage.
macOS: Apple menu > About This Mac > Storage.
If low: empty the Recycle Bin / Trash, delete large unused files, uninstall unused applications, and consider offloading files to an external drive or cloud storage.

STEP 4: Check for malware (Windows)
Malware silently consuming CPU and network resources is a common slowdown cause. Run a full scan with Windows Defender (Settings > Windows Security > Virus & threat protection > Quick scan). Also consider Malwarebytes Free for a second-opinion scan.

STEP 5: Update Windows / macOS and drivers
Pending updates can cause background download/install activity that slows the system temporarily. Complete all pending updates, then restart fully (not sleep or hibernate).

STEP 6: Check thermal throttling
If your laptop is running hot (fan running constantly, base warm to touch), it may be throttling its CPU speed to protect itself from overheating. See our FAQ on laptop overheating for steps to resolve this.

STEP 7: Consider a RAM or storage upgrade
If the machine has 8GB RAM and your Task Manager shows consistently >80% memory usage, upgrading to 16GB (if your laptop supports it) will make a meaningful difference. If your laptop has an HDD rather than SSD, replacing it with an SSD is the single most impactful hardware upgrade possible — the difference is transformative.

If none of these steps help, contact Omnia Retail support with your laptop model and a description of what Task Manager is showing — we can help diagnose further.

FAQ: Does my warranty cover accidental damage?

This is one of the most important warranty questions customers ask — unfortunately, the short answer is: standard warranties do not cover accidental damage. Here is a detailed breakdown:

WHAT STANDARD WARRANTIES COVER
Manufacturer warranties (typically 1–3 years) and your statutory rights under Irish consumer law cover manufacturing defects — faults in materials or workmanship that were present at the time of sale or developed due to a quality issue during normal use. Examples of covered issues: screen backlight failure, keyboard keys stopping working without physical damage, battery not charging when in good condition, SSD failure, random shutdowns with no user error involved.

WHAT STANDARD WARRANTIES DO NOT COVER
- Accidental damage: drops, impacts, cracks, dents
- Liquid damage: spills, rain exposure, condensation ingress
- Screen cracks: one of the most common customer issues — a cracked screen is almost always accidental and not covered by standard warranty
- User-caused damage: modification, repair by non-authorised service centres, opening the case (on sealed devices)
- Theft or loss
- Normal wear and tear: cosmetic scratches from everyday use

ACCIDENTAL DAMAGE PROTECTION OPTIONS
If you want cover for accidents, there are two main routes:

1. Manufacturer AccidentalDamage Protection: Some manufacturers offer paid add-ons:
   - AppleCare+ (€5.99–14.49/month or one-time fee): Covers up to 2 accidental damage incidents per year, subject to a service fee (€29 for screen damage, €99 for other damage). Highly recommended for MacBook users.
   - Dell Premium Support Plus: Includes accidental damage coverage for drops and spills.
   - HP Care Pack with Accidental Damage Protection: Available for most HP EliteBook and Spectre models.

2. Home Insurance: Many home insurance policies (contents cover) include portable electronics as specified items. Check your policy or contact your insurer — adding a laptop as a specified item typically costs €20–50/year in Ireland. This often provides broader cover than manufacturer programmes.

Omnia Retail does not currently offer in-house accidental damage protection plans, but we can advise on manufacturer programme availability for the product you are purchasing.

HOW TO MAKE AN ACCIDENTAL DAMAGE CLAIM
Contact the manufacturer or your insurer directly. Have ready: proof of purchase, serial number, description of the incident, and photos of the damage. Do not attempt to repair accidental damage yourself before making a claim — this can invalidate the claim.

FAQ: Should I buy a Mac or a Windows laptop?

This is genuinely one of the most personal technology decisions, and the right answer depends on your situation. Here is an honest comparison to help you decide:

CHOOSE A MAC IF:
- You are already in the Apple ecosystem (iPhone, iPad, AirPods): Handoff, AirDrop, Universal Clipboard, and iMessage on Mac are genuinely useful and well-integrated.
- You prioritise battery life above all else: The MacBook Air M3 regularly achieves 15–18 hours of real-world battery life. No Windows laptop comes close in the same form factor.
- You do creative work: Apple's M-series chips offer exceptional performance for video editing (Final Cut Pro is Mac-only and highly optimised), music production (Logic Pro is Mac-only), and photography. Adobe Creative Cloud also runs excellently on Apple Silicon.
- You want a premium build quality and display: MacBook displays (Liquid Retina, Retina) are among the best laptop screens available.
- Low maintenance appeals to you: macOS is generally considered more stable and requires less active maintenance than Windows.

CHOOSE WINDOWS IF:
- Your workplace uses Windows software or requires Windows-specific applications, Active Directory, or domain management.
- You play PC games: Windows is the dominant gaming platform; Mac gaming has improved but remains limited by comparison.
- You need specific software only available on Windows: e.g. certain engineering tools, Microsoft Access, older legacy applications.
- Budget is a priority: Windows laptops are available at every price point, including highly capable machines under €600. The cheapest Mac starts at €1,149 (MacBook Air M2).
- You need hardware flexibility: Windows laptops come in hugely varied configurations — different screen sizes, form factors, port selections, upgradeable RAM and SSD in many models.
- You prefer a touchscreen: Most MacBooks do not have touchscreens (iPad notwithstanding). Many Windows laptops and 2-in-1s do.

THE HONEST MIDDLE GROUND
For professional productivity, both platforms are excellent in 2024. Microsoft 365 runs identically on both. Video calls, coding, writing, analysis — all equally capable. The choice increasingly comes down to ecosystem preference, budget, and software requirements rather than raw capability.

Omnia Retail Support Note: If you purchase a Mac and later decide it doesn't suit your needs, our 14-day return policy under the EU Consumer Rights Directive applies.

FAQ: My external hard drive is not showing up on my computer — what should I try?

An external hard drive that doesn't appear can usually be resolved with one of the following steps. Work through them in order:

STEP 1: Try a different USB port and cable
This resolves most cases. USB cables, particularly those supplied with budget drives, fail regularly. Try: a different USB port on your computer (prefer a port directly on the machine over a hub), a different USB cable if you have one available. Also try connecting to a different computer to determine whether the issue is the drive or your computer.

STEP 2: Check if the drive is getting power
For bus-powered drives (no separate power adapter): some USB ports, especially on the back of desktops or older laptops, don't supply sufficient current. Try a USB port on the front panel or a powered USB hub. For drives with a separate power adapter: ensure the adapter is connected and the power LED is lit on the drive.

STEP 3: Check Disk Management (Windows) or Disk Utility (Mac)
The drive may be detected by the operating system but not automatically mounted.

Windows: Right-click Start > Disk Management. If you can see the drive listed but it shows as 'Unallocated' or without a drive letter: right-click > Assign Drive Letter (if the drive has data) or right-click > Format (only if the drive is new or you want to erase it). If it shows as 'Unknown' or 'Not Initialised': this may indicate a more serious drive failure.

macOS: Open Disk Utility (Applications > Utilities). If the drive appears in the sidebar but is greyed out: click it and click Mount. If it appears but cannot be mounted, it may have a filesystem the Mac cannot read (e.g. NTFS from a Windows-formatted drive — Macs can read NTFS by default but not write to it).

STEP 4: Check filesystem compatibility
Windows-formatted drives (NTFS) are readable on Mac but not writable without third-party software (e.g. Paragon NTFS for Mac, €20). Mac-formatted drives (HFS+, APFS) are not readable on Windows without third-party software. For cross-platform use, format the drive as exFAT — natively read/write on both Windows and macOS.

STEP 5: Listen for signs of physical failure
If the drive makes clicking, grinding, or beeping noises, this indicates physical damage to the drive heads or motor. Stop using the drive immediately — continued use risks making data recovery impossible. Do not attempt to open the drive. Contact a professional data recovery service.

STEP 6: Contact support
If none of the above steps resolve the issue and the drive is within warranty, contact Omnia Retail support with your order number. We will advise on warranty replacement options.

FAQ: What is the difference between Wi-Fi 5, Wi-Fi 6, and Wi-Fi 6E?

Wi-Fi standards have evolved significantly. Here is what the different generations mean in practical terms:

WI-FI 5 (802.11ac) — Current mainstream standard
Introduced: 2014
Frequency bands: 2.4GHz and 5GHz
Max theoretical speed: Up to 3.5 Gbps (in ideal multi-antenna configurations)
Real-world speeds: 300–900 Mbps in typical home environments
Suitable for: Standard broadband connections, most home and office use. If your router and devices are Wi-Fi 5, you are unlikely to be the bottleneck unless you have a very fast broadband plan (500Mbps+) or a dense, heavily congested wireless environment.

WI-FI 6 (802.11ax) — Current premium standard
Introduced: 2019
Frequency bands: 2.4GHz and 5GHz (same as Wi-Fi 5, different channels and techniques)
Key improvements over Wi-Fi 5: Higher efficiency in congested environments (OFDMA and MU-MIMO improvements), better performance when many devices connect simultaneously (relevant for smart homes with 20+ devices), improved power management extending battery life on compatible devices.
Max theoretical speed: Up to 9.6 Gbps
Real-world speeds: 600 Mbps–1.5 Gbps in good conditions
Suitable for: Dense home environments, offices with many concurrent devices, anyone with a gigabit broadband connection.

WI-FI 6E (802.11ax with 6GHz extension)
Introduced: 2021
Frequency bands: 2.4GHz, 5GHz, AND 6GHz
Key improvements: The 6GHz band is largely free of existing device interference (no legacy devices use 6GHz), providing a clean, fast channel for compatible devices. Excellent for mesh networking backhaul (the link between mesh nodes).
Range limitation: 6GHz has shorter range and worse wall penetration than 5GHz or 2.4GHz — important consideration in Irish homes with concrete walls (see our mesh networking FAQ).
Suitable for: Power users, multi-device households, anyone building a new whole-home mesh system.

PRACTICAL BUYING ADVICE
For a new router or mesh system in 2024, Wi-Fi 6 is the minimum recommendation — prices are competitive and the longevity benefit is worthwhile. Wi-Fi 6E is worth considering if you are setting up a new mesh system and want the cleanest backhaul channel, though be aware of the concrete wall penetration limitation in older Irish housing.

Device compatibility: Your router's Wi-Fi generation only benefits devices that support the same standard. An older laptop with Wi-Fi 5 will connect to a Wi-Fi 6 router but only at Wi-Fi 5 speeds — backwards compatibility is always maintained.
